title: Writing - Part 1: Celebrity Culture
description: Your class has been discussing the influence of celebrities on modern society.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the influences in your notes. You should explain which influence you find most concerning, giving reasons for your opinion.

influences: Setting unrealistic standards of beauty and success
Using fame to raise awareness of social issues
opinions: Young people are particularly vulnerable to celebrity influence.
Celebrities have a responsibility to use their platform wisely.
The constant pursuit of fame distorts what young people value most.

structure.introduction: Introduce celebrity culture and the two influences you will discuss.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Discuss the first influence (e.g., unrealistic standards of beauty) and how it affects young people.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the second influence (e.g., promoting consumer behaviour) and its wider effects.
structure.conclusion: Evaluate both influences and state which you find most concerning.

content.modelAnswer:
Celebrity culture has become an increasingly dominant force in modern life, with social media allowing stars to reach audiences of millions at the tap of a screen. While celebrities can use their influence positively, there are growing concerns about their impact on young people's values and self-image. This essay will discuss how celebrities set unrealistic standards and how they shape consumer habits.

One of the most damaging aspects of celebrity culture is the promotion of unattainable ideals of beauty and success. When young people constantly see carefully edited images of wealthy, physically perfect celebrities, they may feel inadequate or dissatisfied with their own lives. Studies link heavy social media use with increased rates of anxiety and low self-esteem, particularly among teenage girls.

Additionally, celebrities have enormous power to influence purchasing decisions. Through advertising partnerships and sponsored posts, they encourage their followers to buy products they may not need and often cannot afford. This culture of consumption teaches young people to measure their worth by what they own, rather than who they are.

In conclusion, while both influences are worrying, I believe the promotion of unrealistic standards is the most concerning. It attacks young people's sense of self-worth at a vulnerable period in their development, with consequences that can affect mental health for many years. Society needs to do more to promote realistic and diverse representations of success and beauty.
